content: Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM) is a {{c1::rare congenital disease}}.
Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM) is a progressive form of {{c1::localized gigantism}}.
The estimated birth prevalence of Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM) is about {{c1::one in 100 000 live births}}.
Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM) is characterized by {{c1::lipomatosis of the nerves and disproportionate mesenchymal proliferation of digits in the involved nerve territory}}.
Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM) most often involves {{c1::the second or third digit of the hand or foot}} and is usually {{c2::unilateral}}.
In Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM), radiographs typically show {{c1::soft-tissue and osseous hypertrophy with lengthened, splayed, or expanded distal phalanges}}.
In Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM), CT typically shows {{c1::excessive bone growth and adipose tissue proliferation along the peripheral nerves}}.
In Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM), MRI typically shows {{c1::hypointense fibrous bands in adipose tissue, cortical thickening, and fatty infiltration of the nerves}}.
In symptomatic Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM), management ranges from {{c1::orthotics}} to {{c2::surgical debulking or osteotomy}}.
A proposed molecular cause of Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM) is {{c1::somatic activating PIK3CA mutations}}.

In Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM), what do the arrows indicate on this radiograph?

{{c1::Ankylosis of the distal interphalangeal (DIP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints}}

In Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM), what do the arrows indicate on this MRI?

{{c1::Fatty infiltration of the median nerve in its proximal branches}}

Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM)}}

Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Macrodystrophia lipomatosa macrodactyly (MLM)}}
